original_hypothesis.title: Unset feeWallet (address(0)) Permanently Blocks All Profitable Withdrawal Executions
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: feeWallet defaults to address(0) (never set in constructor). If performanceFeeBps > 0 and feeWallet is never set via setFeeWallet(), any executeWithdrawal where the user has profit (assetsToWithdraw > withdrawnBasis) will attempt SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer to address(0). Most ERC-20 tokens revert on transfer to address(0), permanently blocking all profitable withdrawal executions.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a configuration gap that creates a denial-of-service for all profitable withdrawals. Users who have already burned their shares via requestWithdrawal cannot recover them if the withdrawal execution reverts, effectively locking their funds until the owner sets a valid feeWallet.
deployment_assumptions: feeWallet is never set via setFeeWallet() after deployment
underlying token reverts on transfer to address(0)
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Constructor does not set feeWallet. No deployment script provided to verify post-deploy setup. feeWallet defaults to address(0) in storage.

proof_of_signal.pattern: SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer(underlyingToken, feeWallet, performanceFee) where feeWallet can be address(0); no validation in constructor or executeWithdrawal

preconditions: Underlying token reverts on transfer to address(0)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No feeWallet validation in constructor or executeWithdrawal
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Configuration omission causes DoS on all profitable withdrawals
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit to attacker; victims lose access to withdrawal proceeds proportional to their burned shares

economic_questions: What is the total value of shares that could be burned before the issue is discovered?
Can the owner recover the situation by calling setFeeWallet after the fact?
Are there any ERC-20 tokens that allow transfer to address(0)?
validation_requirements: Verify feeWallet default value is address(0)
Test executeWithdrawal with feeWallet=address(0) and positive performanceFee
Confirm that setting feeWallet after the fact unblocks pending withdrawals

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A - not an attack, configuration gap

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after deployment without proper setup

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users with profitable withdrawal requests
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of their withdrawal is blocked (shares already burned)
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All profitable withdrawal proceeds in the vault

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Verify constructor does not set feeWallet; check deployment scripts
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test revert path on executeWithdrawal with feeWallet=address(0)

validation_result.impact_summary: If performanceFeeBps > 0 and feeWallet is never set (defaults to address(0)), all profitable withdrawal executions revert because most ERC-20 tokens reject transfer to address(0). Users' shares are already burned at requestWithdrawal time, locking their funds until the owner calls setFeeWallet.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The HypoVault constructor (lines ~175-183) sets performanceFeeBps but never sets feeWallet, leaving it at the default address(0). The executeWithdrawal function (lines ~293-340) computes a performance fee when assetsToWithdraw > withdrawnBasis, then calls SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer(underlyingToken, feeWallet, performanceFee) when performanceFee > 0. For standard ERC-20 tokens (including OpenZeppelin's ERC20 which reverts on transfer to address(0)), this call will revert, blocking the entire executeWithdrawal transaction. Since requestWithdrawal already burns user shares via _burnVirtual, users who have requested withdrawals cannot access their assets and cannot recover their burned shares. The owner can remediate by calling setFeeWallet(), but until then all profitable withdrawals are blocked. This is a genuine code defect: a missing constructor initialization and missing runtime validation.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The constructor at lines 175-183 does not set feeWallet. The feeWallet storage variable is declared at line 140 with no default assignment (Solidity default: address(0)). The executeWithdrawal function at lines 328-331 performs SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer to feeWallet without checking feeWallet != address(0). No validation exists anywhere in the withdrawal path to ensure feeWallet is a valid non-zero address before attempting the fee transfer.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: feeWallet is not set in the constructor. When performanceFeeBps > 0, this creates an inconsistent state where fees will be calculated but cannot be transferred.

No validation that feeWallet != address(0) before the transfer. If feeWallet is address(0), this will revert for standard ERC-20 tokens, blocking the entire withdrawal execution including the user's asset transfer on the subsequent line.
Default Solidity value is address(0). No immutable assignment or constructor initialization.

calling_sequence: 1. User calls requestWithdrawal(shares) - shares burned via _burnVirtual, basis moved to queuedWithdrawal
2. Manager calls fulfillWithdrawals() - epoch advances, reservedWithdrawalAssets increased, totalSupply decreased
3. Anyone calls executeWithdrawal(user, epoch) - epoch check passes (epoch < withdrawalEpoch)
4. Performance fee calculated: Math.max(0, int256(assetsToWithdraw) - int256(withdrawnBasis)) * performanceFeeBps / 10_000
5. If performanceFee > 0 (user has profit AND performanceFeeBps > 0): SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer(underlyingToken, feeWallet=address(0), performanceFee)
6. Standard ERC-20 token reverts on transfer to address(0) → entire transaction reverts
7. User's shares were already burned in step 1 and cannot be recovered; withdrawal is stuck
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path from requestWithdrawal through fulfillWithdrawals to executeWithdrawal is the standard withdrawal flow. requestWithdrawal (line 218) calls _burnVirtual (line 237) which decrements balanceOf[user] irreversibly for that epoch. After fulfillWithdrawals advances the epoch, executeWithdrawal (line 293) is called. At line 316-318, the performance fee is computed. At lines 326-329, if performanceFee > 0, SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer is called with feeWallet (which is address(0)). Most ERC-20 tokens (including OpenZeppelin-based ones) have a require(to != address(0)) check in their transfer function, causing a revert. The subsequent safeTransfer to the user at line 331 is never reached.

deployment_assumptions_checked: feeWallet is never set via setFeeWallet() after deployment
underlying token reverts on transfer to address(0)
config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. foundry.toml and remappings.txt contain only build configuration, no deployment or post-deployment setup logic. There is no evidence that setFeeWallet is called after deployment.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an external attack but a configuration-gap DoS. The scenario unfolds as follows: (1) Vault is deployed with performanceFeeBps > 0 but setFeeWallet() is never called. (2) Users deposit and the vault generates profit. (3) Users call requestWithdrawal(), burning their shares. (4) Manager calls fulfillWithdrawals(). (5) Users (or anyone) call executeWithdrawal() but the transaction reverts on the fee transfer to address(0). (6) Users' shares are already burned and cannot be recovered through normal operations. The manager could call cancelWithdrawal for users still in the current epoch, but for fulfilled epochs this is not possible. The owner can resolve this by calling setFeeWallet() with a valid address, after which pending executeWithdrawal calls will succeed. The defect is real: the code fails to enforce that feeWallet is valid when performanceFeeBps > 0.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH severity. All profitable withdrawal executions are blocked when feeWallet == address(0) and performanceFeeBps > 0. Users who called requestWithdrawal have their shares burned and cannot access their underlying assets until the owner intervenes. The remediation path exists (owner calls setFeeWallet) but requires off-chain awareness. In the worst case, if the owner key is lost or the owner is unresponsive, profitable withdrawals are permanently blocked.
recommended_mitigations: Add feeWallet as a constructor parameter when performanceFeeBps > 0, with a require(performanceFeeBps == 0 || _feeWallet != address(0)) check
Add a require(feeWallet != address(0)) check in executeWithdrawal before the fee transfer, or alternatively send the fee to the owner as a fallback
Add address(0) validation in setFeeWallet to prevent setting it back to zero: require(_feeWallet != address(0))
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy HypoVault with performanceFeeBps=1000 (10%) and do NOT call setFeeWallet(). 2. Use a standard ERC-20 token (e.g., OpenZeppelin ERC20). 3. Deposit tokens via requestDeposit. 4. Fulfill deposits via fulfillDeposits at some NAV. 5. Execute deposits to get shares. 6. Increase vault value (simulate profit). 7. Call requestWithdrawal to burn shares. 8. Fulfill withdrawals via fulfillWithdrawals at higher NAV (profit scenario). 9. Call executeWithdrawal and observe revert on SafeTransferLib.safeTransfer to address(0). 10. Verify shares are burned and user cannot recover them. 11. Call setFeeWallet(validAddress) and retry executeWithdrawal to confirm it succeeds.
uncertainties: Some non-standard ERC-20 tokens may allow transfer to address(0) (e.g., tokens without the zero-address check), in which case the fee would be burned rather than causing a revert
The actual deployment process may include a post-deployment setup script that calls setFeeWallet(), which would mitigate this issue in practice
additional_testing_needed: Test with specific ERC-20 tokens planned for use (USDC, WETH, etc.) to confirm they revert on transfer to address(0)
Review any deployment scripts or operational runbooks not included in the codebase
Test the recovery path: verify that calling setFeeWallet() after the fact unblocks all pending executeWithdrawal calls
